cataloging_subject.label: Sir Torrent of Portingale
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English tail-rhyme romance conventionally titled Sir Torrent of Portingale. The intellectual work is distinct from its unique near-complete Chetham manuscript, two independent early-print fragments, Adam's edited reading text and parallel fragment appendix, later scholarly editions, digital encodings, repository XML, and photographic reproductions. Adam's seven fragment groups are alternate witnesses to passages of this same work, not seven intrinsic continuations or chapters.

catalog_summary.title: Sir Torrent of Portingale
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Toward the end of the fourteenth century (often c. 1400); uniquely attested as a near-complete text in a manuscript copied c. 1470-1480; fragmentarily printed c. 1505 and c. 1510
catalog_summary.region: England; probably the North Midlands, with a more specific northern East Midlands localization proposed but not certain

titles.supplied_by: James Wade's current Middle English Texts Series edition
notes: The preferred catalog title follows a current scholarly edition; spelling varies substantially across manuscript and editions.

titles.supplied_by: Erich Adam's 1887 edition and the CME source title page

titles.value: Here bygynneth a good tale / Of Torrente of Portyngale
notes: Line division is retained to distinguish the two-line opening rubric from a modern supplied title.
No medieval author is named. Proposals about a minstrel, household context, or professional background do not establish personal identity.

scope.description: Immediate 1887 source edition represented by CME00056

notes: This is editorial responsibility for the immediate printed expression, not medieval authorship.

scope.description: 1884 manuscript collation and extensive collaboration on Adam's 1887 edition

notes: Adam says Kölbing revised the introduction, notes, and glossary before and after printing and repeatedly proofread the text; Kölbing also published the prior manuscript collation and seventh print fragment.

scope.description: Proof checking for Adam's 1887 edition against the Chetham manuscript

notes: Adam credits Hall with reading proofs against the manuscript and supplying marked notes.
Halliwell is a prior editor, not the immediate CME editor or the medieval author.
Wade's edition supplies current metadata and comparison readings but is not an ancestor of CME00056.

date_statements.display: Toward the end of the fourteenth century; often cataloged c. 1400

assertion.notes: The date is a scholarly estimate, not a dated authorial or scribal statement.

date_statements.display: 1470-1480 (often c. 1475) in Chetham's Library MS Mun.A.6.31

assertion.notes: The repository broadly dates the codex to the fifteenth century; the narrower 1470-1480 range is current scholarly cataloging.

date_statements.display: c. 1505? in a fragmentary Richard Pynson printing

scope.description: Earliest surviving print witness; only two leaves survive

assertion.notes: The date and attribution are conventional and the surviving object is fragmentary.

date_statements.display: 1842, first complete modern publication by James Orchard Halliwell

assertion.notes: This does not erase the earlier fragmentary print witnesses.

places.label: North Midlands
notes: Broad composition and dialect localization used by the Database of Middle English Romance.

places.label: Northern East Midlands near the Lincolnshire-Nottinghamshire-West Riding intersection
notes: A narrower scholarly proposal based on lexical and dialect evidence; not treated as certain.

assertion.notes: A scholarly localization based on dialect and linguistic evidence, not a documentary place of writing.
Wade reports the narrower localization as a scholarly proposal; it should not be converted into a certain settlement-level origin.

scope.description: Primary home court and dynastic setting
Crusade, captivity, tournament, and family-recognition episodes

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English introductions, notes, and glosses belong to editorial editions and are not additional languages of the medieval work.

form_statements.description: Narrative verse in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas, conventionally represented as aab ccb ddb eeb, with metrical damage and missing lines in the surviving witnesses.

subject_statements.label: Quests, combat, and monsters
notes: Includes repeated combats with giants, a dragon, and animal dangers; these are central plot structures rather than incidental motifs.

subject_statements.label: Family separation, exile, recognition, and reunion
Sea voyages, exile, and crusade travel

summaries.value: Torrent, an earl's son in Portugal, loves Princess Desonell, but her father repeatedly tries to prevent the match by sending him on lethal quests. Torrent defeats giants, a dragon, and rival champions across an expansive romance geography. After Desonell bears twins, her father casts mother and children to sea; the boys are separately rescued and raised in Jerusalem and Greece, while Desonell reaches Nazareth. Torrent returns, joins crusading warfare, encounters his sons without recognizing them, and ultimately reunites with his family through tournaments, stories, rings, and other tokens of recognition. The lovers marry, their sons inherit kingdoms, and Torrent is elected emperor.

This record catalogs the medieval romance, not Adam's editorial introduction or apparatus. The main CME poem division and its seven-fragment addendum are two editorial representations of overlapping work passages. The fragment appendix is retained in lineage and source mappings but is not counted as additional narrative continuation.

extent.description: Wade's current critical edition presents 2,671 lines of the romance; the Database of Middle English Romance reports 2,668 lines for the manuscript text. The CME main-poem division contains 2,677 L elements grouped in 236 LG elements, while its seven-fragment addendum contains a further 234 L elements in 7 LG groups. Those XML figures are representation metrics: the addendum duplicates selected passages from independent early prints, and main-text L nodes do not map one-to-one to every scholarly lineation. The unique near-complete manuscript text occupies fols. 76r-119v. No canonical stanza total is asserted because the transmitted poem is damaged and incomplete in many stanzas.

content_parts.label: Sir Torrent of Portingale
content_parts.summary: A continuous tail-rhyme romance tracing Torrent's quests for Desonell, separation from his wife and twin sons, crusading adventures and unrecognized family encounters, recognition and reunion, marriage, dynastic settlement, and imperial elevation.

locators.value: 76r-119v; fol. 119v shared with the following Lamentation of the Virgin
163-250; image 250 is the shared fol. 119v boundary
pp. 1-92 edited reading text; pp. 93-100 seven parallel early-print fragments
BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'] plus BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='addendum']; the addendum is parallel fragment evidence, not narrative continuation

notes: The manuscript does not space the twelve-line stanzas separately, and damage leaves many stanzas incomplete.

Adam's seven fragment groups cover line ranges 462-491, 492-521, 819-851, 917-947, 950-971, 1014-1037, and 1807-1866. They are alternate print witnesses and therefore are not modeled as intrinsic content parts.
Folio 119v remains within the work mapping because it contains Torrent's ending even though the following Lamentation begins on the same surface.

related_works.title: Sir Eglamour of Artois
notes: Wade calls Sir Eglamour the nearest literary analogue and a likely partial source. The record does not identify a particular surviving Eglamour manuscript as Torrent's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Legend of St Eustace
notes: The separated-family, exile, trial, and reunion pattern belongs to the broad Eustace-Constance tradition. Adam compared several analogues, but no direct continental or French original is known.

related_works.title: Le Morte Darthur
notes: Wade reports scholarship proposing that Torrent influenced Malory. This is reception history, not an upstream source of Torrent or CME00056.

evidence.citation: CME00056 repository XML and linked lineage evidence.
evidence.summary: Links the descriptive record to the lineage manifest's audited repository-file evidence for title forms, Middle English language, incipit, main poem and addendum boundaries, fragment headings and ranges, page/folio milestones, and representation-specific structure.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XML node count and ancestor inspection of CME00056.xml.
evidence.locator: count of L, LG, DIV1, PB, NOTE, MILESTONE, HI, and ABBR under the poem and addendum divisions
evidence.summary: Finds 2,677 L and 236 LG descendants in the main poem division, 234 L and 7 LG descendants in the fragment addendum, 101 PB in the document, 1,394 HI, and 139 ABBR. These counts are explicitly treated as encoding metrics rather than canonical work extent.

evidence.citation: James Wade, ed., Sir Torrent of Portingale, Middle English Texts Series (2017).
evidence.summary: Supports the preferred title, anonymous authorship, late-fourteenth-century date, 2,671-line tail-rhyme form, North Midlands linguistic discussion, manuscript hands and context, genres, plot and subjects, source analogues, and modern editorial method.

evidence.citation: James Wade, Sir Torrent of Portingale, Bibliography: Manuscript and Early Printed Editions.
evidence.summary: Supports the Chetham shelfmark and modern Pynson/de Worde fragment identifications, dates, Douce sub-numbers, and STC numbers.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Sir Torrent of Portyngale.
evidence.summary: Supports c. 1400 composition, North Midlands localization, twelve-line tail-rhyme scheme, IMEV 983, manuscript date and folios, two early-print fragments, modern editions, plot, and discovery keywords.

evidence.citation: E. Adam, ed., Torrent of Portyngale, EETS ES 51 (1887).
evidence.summary: Supports the CME alternate title, Adam's editorial role, Kölbing and Hall's contributions, manuscript and fragment use, complete printed boundary, fragment ranges, and Adam's historically situated source and analogue discussion.

evidence.citation: James Orchard Halliwell, ed., Torrent of Portugal (1842).
evidence.summary: Supports the alternate title, Halliwell's editorial role, 1842 first complete modern publication, Chetham transcription, and six-fragment appendix.

evidence.citation: Eugen Kölbing, 'Collationen. V. Torrent of Portugal,' Englische Studien 7 (1884): 344-348.
evidence.summary: Supports Kölbing's collation role, correction of Halliwell, and publication of the seventh early-print fragment.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library LUNA full inventory for Chetham MS Mun.A.6.31.
evidence.summary: Supports the current and historical shelfmarks, fifteenth-century date, physical description, 768-surface inventory, exact work folios and image range, and shared fol. 119v boundary.

open_questions.question: How do Wade's 2,671-line edition, the Database of Middle English Romance's 2,668 manuscript lines, Adam's historical numbering and Halliwell cross-numbers, and the CME main division's 2,677 L elements align line by line?
next_steps: Build a line-level crosswalk from Adam's printed number milestones, Halliwell bracket numbers, Wade's lineation, and XML L boundaries.
Keep the 234 addendum L elements separate because they duplicate selected early-print passages rather than extending the narrative.

open_questions.question: Can the broad North Midlands composition localization be narrowed securely without confusing authorial dialect, scribal dialect, and the production place of the surviving Chetham copy?
next_steps: Consult Montgomery's full dialect study and subsequent linguistic scholarship.
Retain broad and narrow localization claims as separately qualified assertions until authorial and scribal layers can be distinguished.

open_questions.question: Which of Adam's seven fragment groups belongs to the Pynson object and which to the de Worde object under current Bodleian shelfmarks?
next_steps: Obtain or inspect complete recto-verso images of both two-leaf objects.
Reconcile Wade's bibliography with the current SOLO physical record and map every printed group to its source object.
notes: Until resolved, the content model treats the seven groups as parallel witness evidence without assigning them to one physical print object.
The catalog summary's editor names the immediate CME source editor and his extensively credited collaborator; it does not imply joint medieval authorship.

Adam's conjecture of a lost older English poem common to Torrent and Eglamour is retained only as historical editorial interpretation. Current metadata states the supported, narrower claim that Eglamour is the closest analogue and likely partial source.

The Chetham witness is the unique near-complete manuscript, but two detached early-print objects preserve overlapping passages from a separate transmission branch; 'unique witness' is therefore qualified rather than absolute.
